content_markdown: An ideal gas expands isothermally, doing $2500\text{ J}$ of external work in the process. The thermal energy absorbed by the gas in this process is
vibe_explanation: According to the first law of thermodynamics, $Q = \Delta U + W$. For an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas, the temperature remains constant, which means the internal energy change $\Delta U$ is zero. Therefore, the thermal energy absorbed $Q$ must be exactly equal to the work done $W$ by the gas.
